id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0000
text: ![](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/shutterstock-2136788367-landscape-4x1.jpg?t=h440)

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0000
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0001
text: [Report](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/rapporten/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0001
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0002
text: [Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0002
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0003
text: [Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/julia-krauwer/index.html)

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0003
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0004
text: Although four out of five software companies expect AI agents to change the software landscape noticeably, almost one third of businesses are not yet technically ready to embed the technology. Half are in the experimentation phase, and one quarter are not doing anything with agentic AI yet.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0004
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0005
text: A large majority of software companies expect the software landscape to change noticeably due to “AI agents” — smart programs that can perform complex tasks independently. Almost half of respondents even expect far-reaching to fundamental changes in the way software is designed, used or controlled, according to research by ABN AMRO and Panelwizard among 204 Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) companies. SaaS is software taken out on a subscription and offered as a cloud service.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0005
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0006
text: The development of artificial intelligence (AI) is moving from generating text, software code and images within an app to independently running complete work processes. Examples include AI agents that manage inventories, send quotations or prepare and execute a complete social media strategy. Through agentic AI, SaaS companies can make their product more attractive, for example by automating tasks or by putting an AI agent forward as the user’s point of contact.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0006
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0007
text: The term “intelligent agent” was introduced back in the 1980s to describe systems that can perceive, reason and act independently. Due to technological advances, AI agents have become much more powerful in recent years. Through Large Language Models (LLMs), the engine behind tools like ChatGPT, agents have the necessary knowledge about the world, which makes communication smooth. With the rise of so-called “reasoning models”, language models that can reason step by step, agents can also devise strategies themselves and determine the associated intermediate steps. By linking to external applications, AI agents can also actually perform actions, such as launching an online advertising campaign or contacting a customer or supplier.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0007
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0008
text: Aside from the opportunities to improve existing SaaS or create new “AI-first” SaaS, the rise of agents could also influence the market at a deeper level. According to Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella, this means the end of software in its current form. He believes that before long people will no longer work with separate software packages, but with AI agents that independently draw on different data sources and, based on that, carry out the necessary actions. To automatically draw up a customer quotation, the AI agent retrieves information from the customer database, combines it with current product or price information, and, if necessary, adds financial conditions based on previous customer behaviour. After review — or fully autonomously — the quotation is sent automatically by email. Users no longer need to switch between systems themselves, because application-independent agents do the thinking and organising, in Nadella’s view.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0008
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0009
text: AI agents can also create entirely new applications. Claude, the AI assistant from tech company Anthropic, for example, creates immediately usable apps based on a few user instructions. With specialised software such as Lovable, Windsurf and Cursor, people without programming knowledge can also develop software, apps and websites; the AI agent interprets the user’s request and turns it into code — in some tools the result is even visible straight away. Although building good, secure and reliable software is a specialism in its own right, this trend of “vibe coding” could mean that more and more companies choose to develop tools themselves rather than buying them in as SaaS.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0009
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0010
text: The influence of agents will extend across multiple levels. For instance, 75 per cent of respondents assume that the buttons and menus we now see on our screens will at least partly make way for an AI agent as the point of contact. It takes on tasks from the user, executes them in the background and reports back the outcome. Many people now know this form of interaction from smart AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Gemini and Claude.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0010
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0011
text: Samir Selimi, founder of software company PurchAIse, has also put an AI agent at the forefront of his platform. The assistant, called PAI, can handle a wide range of tasks in supplier management, such as validating data, placing orders and renegotiating contracts. Selimi sees the chat with PAI as a smart addition to the traditional user interface, which he believes will remain valuable. “We are used to things being displayed visually when we interact with a computer,” he says. “You do not need forty screens or sixty tabs, but you do need some sort of cockpit where, as a user, you can keep an overview.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0011
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0012
text: The expectation is that communication in natural language — in other words, everyday spoken and written language as people use it themselves — will play an important role within software. “You see a shift in the way users interact with software,” says Jelle Visser of ValueBlue, a platform for enterprise architecture. ValueBlue makes software that allows companies to record, analyse and improve their IT landscape schematically. “If a business analyst wants to understand exactly what such an architecture diagram is telling them, it helps if they can ask that question on our platform in that same way.” At present, 38 per cent of software companies see opportunities for themselves to use an AI agent as the point of contact. Visser himself does not feel there is room to wait. “As a software company, you must reinvent your user interface. If we do not do it, our competitor will.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0012
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0013
text: One fifth of the companies surveyed fear that their user experience will no longer be distinctive as a result. What now still runs via mouse clicks and menu structures can at least partly be replaced by typing or speaking a single sentence. This may put pressure on an important element of software companies’ distinctiveness — design, ease of use and visual branding.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0013
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0014
text: One third of respondents, however, see the user experience as their main source of distinctiveness for the future. Even if interaction with software becomes more conversational, there are still plenty of opportunities to give the AI agent a clear brand identity. Since the early rise of chatbots on corporate websites, experts have been looking at ways to give these digital assistants a personality that matches the brand — for example with a fitting avatar, name and manner of communication.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0014
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0015
text: More than a quarter of SaaS companies see merit in ad hoc generated screens. “You then tell an agent: the window must be green, with corners and no rounding. And suddenly there is a screen, adapted to your preferences,” illustrates Franklin Evers of software company Usere casually. The company wants to keep its front end — the part the user sees — flexible anyway. Co-founder Pim Schipper: “Each employee benefits from a different kind of information. Agentic AI allows everyone to get a tailored dashboard.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0015
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0016
text: Visser of ValueBlue also recognises the tension. “The people who use our software all have different roles and different levels of maturity. But it is a lot of work to program a separate variant of the software for everyone.” In his view, agentic AI can offer a solution here. “By giving an agent the necessary context about a role, it can determine what kind of screens and functions an individual user needs.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0016
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0017
text: Dashboards generated on the spot, or even full applications and games that are spun up from scratch, are not a hypothetical scenario. For example, researchers developed an AI model with which the shooter game Doom can be generated ad hoc. The player encounters enemies and navigates through all kinds of spaces without any software code being needed; there is only an AI model trained on large amounts of visual material from the game. Google built a prototype in which its latest Gemini model creates a user interface, each time based on the events in the previous screen — no menu was fixed in advance.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0017
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0018
text: Of the SaaS companies surveyed, 16 per cent fear that their software will fade into the background — a concern that is not entirely unfounded now that consumers and businesses are increasingly turning to independent AI assistants. ChatGPT and Claude now support so-called “actions” with which they independently perform tasks in other applications, such as sending emails or modifying CRM data. The established order in the tech world is also increasingly positioning itself as coordinating platform players. Microsoft allows companies to build their own AI agents with Copilot Studio, which can call on not only Microsoft apps but also external software. Salesforce developed a similar infrastructure with Agentforce.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0018
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0019
text: One in five software companies are considering a link with such AI agent platforms. One quarter are not focusing on specific platforms, but want to make their own software “agent-ready” in a broader sense. These entrepreneurs make functions available modularly, ensure clear documentation, and set up their software so that instructions from external agents — whether or not in natural language — can easily be translated into concrete actions within the application. “As a software company, you must make sure that LLMs can talk to your back end,” summarises Dennis De Reus of Stellar. He is co-founder of Stellar, a platform that allows companies to develop customer service agents themselves.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0019
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0020
text: Almost one third of companies are focusing more broadly on improving data access, integration options and standardised communication with other systems (“interoperability”). Among them is Last Mile Solutions, a software platform for service providers in electric transport — such as charge point operators — that provides technology for, among other things, payments, fraud detection, and preventing overloading of the power grid. A lot of data is needed to provide these services, such as information about the amount of green energy generated at a given time, and how full individual car batteries are.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0020
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0021
text: “Those data can be used by other parties to develop AI models and specialised agents,” says Marketing Director Ben Ridgway. “So we provide a ‘data backbone’ that enables AI innovations, such as smart charging and predictive maintenance.” Ridgway of Last Mile Solutions, which acts as a central hub in the field of e-mobility software, fully agrees. “Our back end is extremely complex. There is so much data that we manage and must make available in the right way. For us, it is crucial to demonstrate time and again that we are a reliable partner.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0021
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0022
text: AI agents do not necessarily operate within the boundaries of an application, but can also switch between separate databases and tools to carry out tasks independently. For example, an agent that retrieves historical sales data from system A, predicts future sales based on an algorithm from system B and displays the associated chart in a dashboard of system C. It is therefore less about the total package, and more about how individual pieces of software can be accessed by AI systems.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0022
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0023
text: Even before the rise of AI agents, many software companies were working to break their systems into small, reusable modules and make them available via so-called APIs or SDKs. Many AI models are also offered this way, so that developers can, for example, embed a smart chatbot on their own website. “Building software is increasingly a matter of combining all kinds of components,” says Visser of ValueBlue. “As a result, entering the software market is easier than ever.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0023
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0024
text: This applies equally to AI agents, which can draw à la carte on data and functions from different providers. Only one quarter of SaaS companies, however, have the preconditions in order for this. They have the necessary digital connections in the form of APIs — short for application programming interfaces — or are sometimes even explicitly designed to work with AI agents and agent platforms.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0024
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0025
text: If software companies make separate elements of their systems widely available, it can be quite difficult for external agents to determine each time which function they take from which party. Sebastiaan den Boer, Director of AI and Data Science at digital agency Youwe: “If you have to keep making an AI agent choose between twenty tools and fifteen databases, then you must also capture the knowledge needed for that in a prompt. That can soon become a very complex instruction.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0025
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0026
text: Some companies (14 per cent) see the risk that specific functions in their software will become redundant because externally developed AI agents perform tasks in their own way, without using existing software modules. One example is an agent that segments customers itself, instead of calling a built-in filter function in existing software. This concern is limited among companies with annual turnover of more than 25 million euros (7 per cent), while one quarter of smaller companies fear this.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0026
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0027
text: Another 9 per cent fear that their carefully built logic or AI models will no longer be used, for example when agents developed elsewhere can make decisions based on their own reasoning abilities. For instance, a recruitment agent could assess applicants using its own scoring method, whereby the internal matching model of the software is completely ignored. This previously purchased HR software then serves only as a data source, or is even bypassed entirely.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0027
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0028
text: De Reus of Stellar is less afraid of such a scenario. He can imagine that software users will interact more and more with AI agents, but is convinced that the underlying systems will often remain relevant. “For example, an agent cannot just store data at random. You need a system to ensure that this happens according to certain structures and rules. The same applies to customer identification; you need modules that allow you to establish someone’s identity with certainty, and then rules that determine which data a particular customer may or may not view.” According to Franklin Evers, co-founder of Usere, this is exactly where software companies distinguish themselves. “It is precisely in that smart middle layer that your intellectual property sits, and you must protect it,” says Evers, who, together with partner Pim Schipper, is developing an AI-driven marketing solution.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0028
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0029
text: Companies with fundamental concerns about their business model are in the minority. Software companies smell opportunities to improve their own product with AI agents. Two thirds of respondents see merit in agents that perform tasks on behalf of the user, such as retrieving information, or running processes that previously happened based on strict steps and rules. Six in ten companies would like to deploy AI agents to support their own customers.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0029
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0030
text: For example, PurchAIse uses agentic AI for supplier management, including retrieving and providing market insights. An automatically detected price drop of ingredients can be a reason to renegotiate a contract, for which AI agent PAI then also drafts an email to the supplier. Usere is initially focusing on marketing and is relieving SMEs by automating, among other things, the creation and placement of social media posts.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0030
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0031
text: Among respondents there is concern about the potential unpredictability of AI agents. Almost half of software companies see a lack of transparency and control as a real risk. AI agents do not work according to fixed rules, but on the basis of probability; they choose the most likely next step and its content, but not necessarily the right one.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0031
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0032
text: “It is hard to set up processes well and predictably with agentic AI,” says Rogier Hosman, co-founder of digital agency Youwe, which develops digital solutions for companies. Hosman knows from experience what is needed for a successful implementation. “Do I have sufficient control over the technology, or will half of my SaaS product collapse? What risk am I willing to take in that?” Although concrete rules can also be given here to steer the agent, it is a fundamentally different way of working from traditional software.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0032
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0033
text: Although software companies see countless opportunities in the field of agentic AI, the question is whether they can take the necessary steps in the short term. Nearly 30 per cent of respondents doubt whether they are technically ready for AI agents; their software architecture or organisation is not yet sufficiently set up for the application of and integration with agents. It is striking that the smallest companies, with annual turnover of less than 2 million euros, see this risk much less — possibly because their IT landscape is less complex and therefore more flexible.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0033
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0034
text: It is also striking that a large proportion of software companies (53 per cent) are still in the experimentation phase with AI agents. These companies are exploring the technology, but have not yet embedded it structurally in their product. More than one quarter do not yet have any concrete plans in the field of agentic AI at all. Maturity in the field of agentic AI appears to be highly dependent on company size; there is hardly any large company (turnover greater than 25 million euros) that is not yet doing anything with the technology, whereas within micro-companies (turnover less than 2 million euros) this applies to 40 per cent. Vertical software companies, which focus on specific sectors, are also on average less advanced than software companies with a broader orientation.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0034
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0035
text: This limited maturity is worrying, because in the meantime a new generation of technology companies is storming the market. In 2024, according to data from Dealroom, around 3.2 billion dollars was invested worldwide in start-ups that have embedded agentic AI in their service. This is exponential growth compared to the previous years, which recorded investments of 727 million and 1.3 billion respectively.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0035
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0036
text: Incidentally, the customers of SaaS companies are also far from all ready for large-scale application of AI agents, observes Selimi of PurchAIse. “In theory, technology can do everything, but in practice companies find it difficult to adapt. It is mostly people and processes where things break down.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0036
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0037
text: Amid this technological transformation, software companies cling to the relationship they have built with their customers. More than half — and within large companies even two thirds — expect to retain the most distinctiveness here in the long term, for example due to a deep understanding of the client’s context or collaboration on implementation and further development of the software. Schipper of Usere is on the same page and even thinks there is room for improvement. “You must invest in the relationship with your customer. For a long time, many software companies have failed to really listen to their customer.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0037
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0038
text: For the time being, AI often lacks context that is essential in specialist sectors. That is why a significant proportion of software companies also expect to be able to fall back on domain knowledge in the future, such as relevant laws and regulations or sector-specific processes. “In the end, you need domain expertise to really add value,” says Hosman of Youwe. Selimi of PurchAIse agrees. “One way to differentiate yourself as a software company is to know your end market and the associated processes inside out.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0038
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0039
text: This awareness is logically stronger among vertical software companies (47 per cent), which already focus on specific niches, than among horizontal players (32 per cent). Among large companies, too, a higher percentage (55 per cent) expect to remain distinctive through deep domain knowledge than among SMEs and micro-companies, where the percentage is around one third.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0039
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0040
text: The “soft” elements of customer relationship and domain knowledge are followed by concrete matters that are directly visible in the software: the functional coverage or feature set of the software, and the user experience — both cited by one third of respondents as points of distinctiveness in the long term.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0040
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0041
text: Conversely, the technical matters in the background — data, integration capability and algorithms — rank lowest. According to Schipper and Evers of Usere, software companies would do well to keep investing under the bonnet. Evers: “You must keep the core of your software very robust. Other parties can then talk to it via an API. They must feel that they can build on your technology, can trust it.” Ridgway of Last Mile Solutions, which acts as a spider in the web in the field of e-mobility software, fully agrees. “Our back end is extremely complex. There is so much data that we manage and must make available in the right way. For us, it is essential to demonstrate time and again that we are a reliable partner.”

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0041
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0042
text: SaaS companies agree: the software market will change significantly in the coming years. They will be able to optimise their own software with AI agents, as a point of contact or in the background. Meanwhile, the well-known chat assistants are gaining an increasingly prominent position. As they become the daily starting point for more and more users and integrate further with all kinds of tools, the chance grows that existing software companies will, in one way or another, become less visible.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0042
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0043
text: The rise of platforms that allow users to build agents or software themselves can also mean that activities previously supported by software packages are now handled by individually fabricated AI agents or self-built software. Some software companies see merit in an offensive strategy in which they themselves play a platform role, or rely on the unique added value of (elements of) their software.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0043
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0044
text: A black-and-white scenario in which SaaS is completely wiped out by AI agents developed elsewhere is highly unlikely. However, the new reality requires considerable adaptability from software companies; links with external tools and parties are more important than ever, and making the necessary automation leap yourself is essential to remain relevant.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0044
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents

id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0045
text: Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest-growing sectors. Helped by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector underpins the digital renewal of other sectors.

metadata.chunk_id: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents__a2-late-chunking__0045
metadata.source_slug: Softwarebedrijven nog niet klaar voor AI-agents
metadata.source_title: Software companies not yet ready for AI agents
